revision.text:
[[ar:بينزايتين]][[ban:Benzaiten]][[bcl:Benzaiten]][[bn:বেন্‌যাইতেন]][[de:Benzaiten]][[el:Μπενζάιτεν]][[en:Benzaiten]][[es:Benzaiten]][[eu:Benzaiten]][[fr:Benzaiten]][[he:בנזייטן]][[hi:बेंज़ाइतेन]][[hr:Benten]][[id:Benzaiten]][[it:Benzaiten]][[ja:弁才天]][[ko:변재천]][[mni:ꯕꯦꯟꯓꯥꯢꯇꯦꯟ]][[ms:Benzaiten]][[pl:Benzaiten]][[ru:Бэндзайтэн]][[sh:Benzaiten]][[simple:Benzaiten]][[sv:Benten]][[ta:பென்சைட்டென்]][[th:เบ็นไซเต็ง]][[tr:Benzaiten]][[uk:Бендзайтен]][[uz:Benzaiten]][[zh:辯才天]] {{short description|Japanese Buddhist goddess who originated from the Hindu goddess Saraswati|qq=}} {{about|the goddess|the music album|Osamu Kitajima|qq=}} {{Redirect|Benten|qq=}}{{See also|Hinduism in Japan|Saraswati|qq=}}{{Infobox deity | type = Buddhist | name = Benzaiten | deity_of = Goddess of all that flows: water, music, arts, love, wisdom, wealth, fortune | member_of = the [[Seven Lucky Gods]] | image = 騎龍弁財天-Benzaiten (Goddess of Music and Good Fortune) Seated on a White Dragon MET DP135895.jpg | caption = Benzaiten with a {{ill|Lute|en|Lute|qid=Q180733|lt=Lute|1=Lute}} (''{{ill|Biwa|en|Biwa|qid=Q829729|lt=Biwa|1=Biwa}}'') seated on a white {{ill|Japanese dragon|en|Japanese dragon|qid=Q2366503|lt=dragon|1=Japanese dragon}} | other_names = Benzaitennyo (弁才天女)
Daibenzaiten (大弁才天)
Benten (弁天)
Myōonten (妙音天)
Bionten (美音天)
Sarasabakutei (薩羅婆縛底)
Sarasabattei (薩羅薩伐底)
Sarasantei (薩羅酸底) | script_name = Japanese | script = 弁才天, 弁財天 (''{{ill|Shinjitai|en|Shinjitai|qid=Q1055887|lt=Shinjitai|1=Shinjitai}}'')
辯才天, 辨才天, 辨財天 (''{{ill|Kyūjitai|en|Kyūjitai|qid=Q1147857|lt=Kyūjitai|1=Kyūjitai}}'') | affiliation = {{ill|Deva (Buddhism)|en|Deva (Buddhism)|qid=Q4089509|lt=Deva|1=Deva (Buddhism)}}
[[Lotus Sutra#Chapters 23–28|Gadgadasvara Bodhisattva]] (assumed traits of)
[[Kisshōten]] (assumed traits of)
[[Munakata Taisha|Ichikishima-hime]] (conflated with)
[[Ugajin]] (conflated with) | mantra = ''Oṃ Sarasvatyai [[:en:Svāhā|Svāhā]]''
(''On Sorasobateiei sowaka'') | animals = {{ill|Snake|en|Snake|qid=Q2102|lt=Snake|1=Snake}}, {{ill|Japanese dragon|en|Japanese dragon|qid=Q2366503|lt=dragon|1=Japanese dragon}} | symbols = {{ill|Lute|en|Lute|qid=Q180733|lt=Lute|1=Lute}} (''{{ill|Biwa|en|Biwa|qid=Q829729|lt=Biwa|1=Biwa}}''), sword, ''[[Cintamani|cintāmaṇi]]'' | consort = None
[[Daikokuten]] (some traditions) }} '''Benzaiten''' (''{{ill|Shinjitai|en|Shinjitai|qid=Q1055887|lt=Shinjitai|1=Shinjitai}}'': or ; ''{{ill|Kyūjitai|en|Kyūjitai|qid=Q1147857|lt=Kyūjitai|1=Kyūjitai}}'': 辯才天, 辨才天, or 辨財天, lit. "{{ill|Deva (Buddhism)|en|Deva (Buddhism)|qid=Q4089509|lt=goddess|1=Deva (Buddhism)}} of eloquence", '''Benten''', {{ill|Chinese language|en|Chinese language|qid=Q7850|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese language}}: 辯才天, '''Biancaitian''') is an {{ill|East Asian Buddhism|en|East Asian Buddhism|qid=Q2905453|lt=East Asian Buddhist|1=East Asian Buddhism}} goddess (technically a {{ill|Dharmapala|en|Dharmapala|qid=Q1207630|lt=Dharmapala|1=Dharmapala}}, "Dharma protector") who originated from the {{ill|Hindus|en|Hindus|qid=Q10090|lt=Hindus|1=Hindus}} deity [[Saraswati]]; goddess of speech, the arts, and learning. Worship of Benzaiten arrived in Japan during the sixth through eighth centuries, mainly via {{ill|Classical Chinese|en|Classical Chinese|qid=Q37041|lt=Classical Chinese|1=Classical Chinese}} translations of the ''{{ill|Golden Light Sutra|en|Golden Light Sutra|qid=Q391694|lt=Golden Light Sutra|1=Golden Light Sutra}}'' ({{ill|Sanskrit|en|Sanskrit|qid=Q11059|lt=Sanskrit|1=Sanskrit}}: ''Suvarṇaprabhāsa Sūtra''), which has a section devoted to her.{{cite book |last1=Ludvik |first1=Catherine |title=Sarasvatī: Riverine Goddess of Knowledge. From the Manuscript-carrying Vīṇā-player to the Weapon-wielding Defender of the Dharma |date=2007 |publisher=Brill|pages=1–3|qq=}} Benzaiten was also syncretized with Japanese [[kami]], and adopted into the [[:en:Shinto religion|Shinto religion]], and there are several {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} dedicated to her. As such, Benzaiten is now also associated with dragons, snakes, local Japanese deities, wealth, fortune, protection from disease and danger, and the protection of the state. == Indian deity == [[File:Raja Ravi Varma, Goddess Saraswati.jpg|thumb|left|Saraswati by {{ill|Raja Ravi Varma|en|Raja Ravi Varma|qid=Q333453|lt=Raja Ravi Varma|1=Raja Ravi Varma}}]] [[File:Eight-Armed Benzaiten - 八臂弁才天.jpg|thumb|Benzaiten with eight arms holding a bow, an arrow, a sword, a spear, an axe, a single-pronged {{ill|Vajra|en|Vajra|qid=Q695709|lt=Vajra|1=Vajra}}, a {{ill|Chakram|en|Chakram|qid=Q971797|lt=wheel|1=Chakram}}, and a [[Pasha (Hinduism)|noose]]]] {{see also|Saraswati|qq=}} Saraswati (Sanskrit: ''Sarasvatī''; {{ill|Pali|en|Pali|qid=Q36727|lt=Pali|1=Pali}}: ''Sarassatī'') was originally in the ''{{ill|Rigveda|en|Rigveda|qid=Q727413|lt=Rigveda|1=Rigveda}}'' a river goddess, the deification of the [[:en:Sarasvati River|Sarasvati River]]. She was identified with Vach (Skt. ''{{ill|Vāc|en|Vāc|qid=Q2427516|lt=Vāc|1=Vāc}}''), the Vedic goddess of speech, and from there became considered to be the patron of music and the arts, knowledge, and learning.{{cite book |last1=Kinsley |first1=David |title=Hindu Goddesses: Visions of the Divine Feminine in the Hindu Religious Tradition |date=1998 |publisher=Motilal Banarsidass Publishers |pages=10–13|qq=}}Ludvik (2007). pp. 35-39.{{cite book |last1=Faure |first1=Bernard |title=Protectors and Predators: Gods of Medieval Japan, Volume 2 |date=2015 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |page=164|qq=}} In addition to their association with eloquence and speech, both Saraswati and Vach also show warrior traits: Saraswati for instance was called the "{{ill|Vritra|en|Vritra|qid=Q797498|lt=Vritra|1=Vritra}}-slayer" (''Vṛtraghnī'') in the ''Rigveda'' (6.61.7) and was associated with the {{ill|Maruts|en|Maruts|qid=Q1906028|lt=Maruts|1=Maruts}}.Faure (2015). pp. 164-165.Ludvik (2007). p. 48.{{cite web |author=Griffith, Ralph T.H. (trans.) |title=Rig Veda, Book 6: Hymn LXI. Sarasvatī. |url=https://www.sacred-texts.com/hin/rigveda/rv06061.htm |website=Sacred Texts |access-date=2022-05-21|qq=}} She was also associated with the {{ill|Ashvins|en|Ashvins|qid=Q726593|lt=Ashvins|1=Ashvins}}, with whom she collaborates to bolster {{ill|Indra|en|Indra|qid=Q128335|lt=Indra|1=Indra}}'s strength by telling him how to kill the {{ill|Asura|en|Asura|qid=Q624083|lt=Asura|1=Asura}} [[Namuchi]]. In a hymn in Book 10 of the ''Rigveda'' (10.125.6), Vach declares: "I bend the bow for {{ill|Rudra|en|Rudra|qid=Q855310|lt=Rudra|1=Rudra}} that his arrow may strike and slay the hater of devotion. I rouse and order battle for the people, and I have penetrated Earth and Heaven."{{cite web |author=Griffith, Ralph T.H. (trans.) |title=Rig Veda, Book 10: Hymn CXXV. Vāk. |url=https://www.sacred-texts.com/hin/rigveda/rv10125.htm |website=Sacred Texts |access-date=2022-05-21|qq=}} Saraswati, like many other Hindu deities, was eventually adopted into Buddhism, figuring mainly in {{ill|Mahayana|en|Mahayana|qid=Q48362|lt=Mahayana|1=Mahayana}} texts. In the 15th chapter of [[:en:Yijing|Yijing]]'s translation of the ''[[:en:Sutra of Golden Light|Sutra of Golden Light]]'' (''Suvarṇaprabhāsa Sūtra'') into {{ill|Classical Chinese|en|Classical Chinese|qid=Q37041|lt=Classical Chinese|1=Classical Chinese}} ([[:en:Taishō Tripitaka|Taishō Tripitaka]] 885), Saraswati (大辯才天女, {{ill|Pinyin|en|Pinyin|qid=Q42222|lt=Pinyin|1=Pinyin}}: ''Dàbiàncáitiānnǚ''; Japanese: ''Daibenzaitennyo'', lit. "great goddess of eloquence") appears before the {{ill|The Buddha|en|The Buddha|qid=Q9441|lt=The Buddha|1=The Buddha}}'s assembly and vows to protect all those who put their faith in the sutra, recite it, or copy it. In addition, she promises to increase the intelligence of those who recite the sutra so that they will be able to understand and remember various ''{{ill|Dharani|en|Dharani|qid=Q1207590|lt=Dharani|1=Dharani}}s''. She then teaches the assembly various mantras with which one can heal all illnesses and escape all manner of misfortune. One of the Buddha's disciples, the [[brahmin]] {{ill|Kaundinya|en|Kaundinya|qid=Q2708070|lt=Kaundinya|1=Kaundinya}}, then praises Saraswati, comparing her to Vishnu's consort Narayani ({{ill|Lakshmi|en|Lakshmi|qid=Q131443|lt=Lakshmi|1=Lakshmi}}) and declaring that she can manifest herself not only as a benevolent deity, but also as {{ill|Yamuna in Hinduism|en|Yamuna in Hinduism|qid=Q2348057|lt=Yami|1=Yamuna in Hinduism}}, the sister of {{ill|Yama (Buddhism)|en|Yama (Buddhism)|qid=Q2089602|lt=Yama|1=Yama (Buddhism)}}. He then describes her eight-armed form with all its attributes — bow, arrow, sword, spear, axe, {{ill|Vajra|en|Vajra|qid=Q695709|lt=Vajra|1=Vajra}}, {{ill|Chakram|en|Chakram|qid=Q971797|lt=iron wheel|1=Chakram}}, and [[Pasha (Hinduism)|noose]].{{cite web |title=金光明最勝王經 第7卷 |url=https://tripitaka.cbeta.org/T16n0665_007 |website=CBETA Chinese Electronic Tripiṭaka Collection (漢文大藏經) |access-date=2022-05-21|qq=}}Faure (2015). pp. 165-166. The poem describes Saraswati as one who "has sovereignty in the world", as one who is "good fortune, success, and peace of mind". It also states that she fights in battlefields and is always victorious.{{cite journal |last1=Ludvik |first1=Catherine |date=2004 |title=A Harivaṃśa Hymn in Yijing's Chinese Translation of the Sutra of Golden Light |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/4132114 |journal=Journal of the American Oriental Society |volume=124 |issue=4 |pages=707–734 |doi=10.2307/4132114 |jstor=4132114|qq=}} One key concern of the ''Golden Light Sutra'' is the protection of the state, and as such, Saraswati here also takes on some form of a warrior goddess, similar to {{ill|Durga|en|Durga|qid=Q382973|lt=Durga|1=Durga}}.Ludvik (2007). pp. 265-267. {{ill|Bernard Faure|en|Bernard Faure|qid=Q2897888|lt=Bernard Faure|1=Bernard Faure}} also notes that the Vach already had martial attributes, which may have been retained in some form.Faure (2015). pp. 168-169. == Bencaitian / Benzaiten == [[File:Benzaiten Surrounded by the Goddesses Kariteimo and Kenrochijin and Two Divine Generals, from Kichijoten shrine, c. 1212.jpg|thumb|Eight-armed Benzaiten surrounded by the goddesses Kariteimo ({{ill|Hariti|en|Hariti|qid=Q1353280|lt=Hariti|1=Hariti}}) and Kenrōchijin ({{ill|Prithvi|en|Prithvi|qid=Q1341130|lt=Prithvi|1=Prithvi}}) and two divine generals ({{Circa|1212|qq=}})]] Saraswati became the Chinese 辯才天 (Bencaitian) or "great eloquence deity" (大辯天). This became the Japanese 弁財天 (Benzaiten). In East Asian Buddhism, she is one of the {{ill|Twenty-Four Protective Deities|en|Twenty-Four Protective Deities|qid=Q15912611|lt=Twenty-Four Protective Deities|1=Twenty-Four Protective Deities}} ({{ill|Chinese language|en|Chinese language|qid=Q7850|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese language}}: 二十四諸天; {{ill|Pinyin|en|Pinyin|qid=Q42222|lt=Pinyin|1=Pinyin}}: ''Èrshísì Zhūtiān''). She remained associated with wealth, music, and eloquence and also took on aspects of a fierce protector of the state (due to the influence of the ''Golden Light Sutra'' which promises to protect a country where the sutra is chanted). During the medieval period onwards, Benzaiten came to be associated or even [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|conflated with]] a number of Buddhist and local deities, including the goddess [[Kisshōten]] whose role as goddess of fortune eventually became ascribed to Benzaiten in popular belief). As such, she was eventually also worshiped as a bestower of monetary fortune and became part of the set of popular deities known as the [[Seven Lucky Gods]] (''shichifukujin''). Benzaiten is depicted a number of ways in Japanese art. She is often depicted holding a ''{{ill|Biwa|en|Biwa|qid=Q829729|lt=Biwa|1=Biwa}}'' (a traditional Japanese lute) similar to how Saraswati is depicted with a {{ill|Veena|en|Veena|qid=Q959769|lt=Veena|1=Veena}} in Indian art, though she may also be portrayed wielding a sword and a wish-granting jewel (''[[cintamani|cintāmaṇi]]''). An iconographic formula showing Benzaiten with eight arms holding a variety of weapons (based on the ''Golden Light Sutra'') meanwhile is believed to derive from Durga's iconography. As Uga Benzaiten, she may also be shown with Ugajin (a human-headed white snake) above her head. Lastly, she is also portrayed (albeit rarely) with the head of a snake or a dragon. Benzaiten's worship also spread to Taiwan during the Japanese colonial period, and she is still venerated in certain locations in Taiwan, such as the {{ill|Xian Dong Yan|en|Xian Dong Yan|qid=Q28416709|lt=Xian Dong Yan|1=Xian Dong Yan}} temple in {{ill|Keelung|en|Keelung|qid=Q249996|lt=Keelung City|1=Keelung}}.{{cn|date=October 2024|qq=}} === Esotericism === In Japanese Buddhism, Benzaiten also developed various esoteric aspects. For example, in the {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|qid=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} school's ''Keiran shūyō shū'' 渓嵐拾葉 集 [Collection of Leaves Gathered in Tempestuous Brooks] , a fourteenth century compendium, Benzaiten is associated with the three truths and the three contemplations in one thought, as well as various esoteric [[Bīja|seed syllables]], the unity of the [[Mandala of the Two Realms|two-world mandalas]] in the {{ill|Susiddhikara Sūtra|en|Susiddhikara Sūtra|qid=Q11625192|lt=Susiddhi|1=Susiddhikara Sūtra}} Dainichi (Vairocana Buddha), which represents the ultimate principle of enlightenment.{{Cite journal |last=Faure |first=Bernard (著) |date=2021 |title=From Tiantaishan to Hieizan: The View from the Keiran Shūyō Shū 渓嵐拾葉集 |url=https://buddhism.lib.ntu.edu.tw/DLMBS/jp/search/search_detail.jsp?seq=639990 |journal=Hualin International Journal of Buddhist Studies |language=en |volume=4 |issue=1 |pages=215–255|qq=}} === Syncretism with Shinto kami === Due to her status as a water deity, Benzaiten was also linked with ''[[:en:Nāgas|Nāgas]]'', {{ill|Japanese dragon|en|Japanese dragon|qid=Q2366503|lt=dragons|1=Japanese dragon}}, and [[:en:Snakes|Snakes]]. Over time, Benzaiten became identified with the Japanese snake ''[[kami]]'' [[Ugajin]]. She also became identified with the ''kami'' [[Munakata Taisha|Ichikishima-hime]]. Benzaiten was also adopted as a female ''[[kami]]'' in [[Shinto]], with the name {{nihongo|Ichikishima-hime-no-mikoto|市杵島姫命|qq=}}.{{cite book|last=Bocking|first=Brian|title=A Popular Dictionary of Shinto - 'Benzaiten' |year=1997|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-0-7007-1051-5|qq=}} This kami is one of three ''[[kami]]'' believed to be daughters of the sun goddess [[Amaterasu]], the ancestress of the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=imperial family|1=Imperial House of Japan}}. She is also believed by {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|qid=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} Buddhists to be the essence of the ''kami'' [[Ugajin]], whose effigy she sometimes carries on her head together with a ''[[torii]]'' (see photo below).{{EOS|Ugajin|200|Itō, Satoshi|August 15, 2011|qq=}} As a consequence, she is sometimes also known as {{nihongo|Uga|宇賀|qq=}} Benzaiten or Uga Benten.Ludvik, Catherine. “Uga-Benzaiten: The Goddess and the Snake.” Impressions, no. 33, 2012, pp. 94–109. JSTOR, JSTOR, www.jstor.org/stable/42597966. === Bīja and mantra === [[File:Siddham su.svg|thumb|130px|सु (''su''), Benzaiten's seed syllable (''[[bīja]]'') in {{ill|Siddhaṃ script|en|Siddhaṃ script|qid=Q250379|lt=Siddhaṃ script|1=Siddhaṃ script}}]] The ''[[bīja]]'' or seed syllable used to represent Benzaiten in Japanese esoteric Buddhism is '''{{IAST|su|qq=}}''' (सु, traditionally read in Japanese as '''''so'''''), written in {{ill|Siddhaṃ script|en|Siddhaṃ script|qid=Q250379|lt=Siddhaṃ script|1=Siddhaṃ script}}.{{cite web |title=弁財天 (Benzaiten) |url=http://tobifudo.jp/butuzo/7god/benten.html |website=Flying Deity Tobifudō (Ryūkō-zan Shōbō-in Official Website) |access-date=2022-05-22|qq=}} In Japanese esoteric Buddhism ({{ill|Mikkyō|en|Mikkyō|qid=Q336102|lt=mikkyo|1=Mikkyō}}), Benzaiten's main {{ill|Mantra|en|Mantra|qid=Q131510|lt=Mantra|1=Mantra}} is as follows:{{Cite web |title=Goddess Benzaiten, A-to-Z Dictionary of Japanese Buddhist / Shinto Statues |url=https://www.onmarkproductions.com/html/benzaiten.shtml |access-date=2023-11-04 |website=www.onmarkproductions.com|qq=}} {| class="wikitable" |+ !Sanskrit !Sino-Japanese pronunciation !{{ill|Hiragana|en|Hiragana|qid=Q48332|lt=Hiragana|1=Hiragana}} |- |''[[:en:Oṃ|Oṃ]] Sarasvatyai [[:en:Svāhā|Svāhā]]''Saroj Kumar Chaudhuri (2003). ''Hindu Gods and Goddesses in Japan,'' p. 54. Vedams eBooks (P) Ltd. |''On Sarasabatei-ei Sowaka'' |おん　さらさばていえい　そわか |} ==Temples and shrines== [[File:Benzaiten at Hogonji, Chikubushima-弁才天.jpg|thumb|Benzaiten statue, Hogonji in Nagahama, Shiga prefecture, Japan]] [[File:Hogonji13s3200.jpg|thumb|Eight armed Uga Benzaiten, Hogonji]] In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, the places of worship dedicated to Benzaiten are often called "辯天堂" ({{ill|Benten-dō|en|Benten-dō|qid=Q2896738|lt=Benten-dō|1=Benten-dō}}) or {{nihongo|benten-sha|弁天社|qq=}}. {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}}s dedicated to her are also called by this name. Entire Shinto shrines can be dedicated to her, as in the case of Kamakura's {{ill|Zeniarai Benzaiten Ugafuku Shrine|en|Zeniarai Benzaiten Ugafuku Shrine|qid=Q642681|lt=Zeniarai Benzaiten Ugafuku Shrine|1=Zeniarai Benzaiten Ugafuku Shrine}} or Nagoya's {{ill|Kawahara Shrine|en|Kawahara Shrine|qid=Q3194327|lt=Kawahara Shrine|1=Kawahara Shrine}}. Benzaiten temples or shrines places are commonly located near bodies of water like rivers, ponds, or springs due to her association with water. Benzaiten's worship became integrated with native Japanese beliefs, including serpent and dragon symbolism, as she was originally a river goddess. Benzaiten is enshrined on numerous locations throughout Japan; for example, the {{ill|Enoshima|en|Enoshima|qid=Q989803|lt=Enoshima|1=Enoshima}} Island in {{ill|Sagami Bay|en|Sagami Bay|qid=Q1061221|lt=Sagami Bay|1=Sagami Bay}}, the {{ill|Chikubu Island|en|Chikubu Island|qid=Q2975401|lt=Chikubu Island|1=Chikubu Island}} in {{ill|Lake Biwa|en|Lake Biwa|qid=Q200239|lt=Lake Biwa|1=Lake Biwa}} and the [[Itsukushima]] Island in [[Seto Inland Sea]] (Japan's Three Great Benzaiten Shrines); and she and a five-headed {{ill|Chinese dragon|en|Chinese dragon|qid=Q215605|lt=dragon|1=Chinese dragon}} are the central figures of the {{ill|Enoshima Engi|en|Enoshima Engi|qid=Q3038766|lt=Enoshima Engi|1=Enoshima Engi}}, a history of the shrines on Enoshima written by the Japanese Buddhist monk {{ill|Kōkei (monk)|en|Kōkei (monk)|qid=Q6455194|lt=Kōkei|1=Kōkei (monk)}} (皇慶) in 1047. According to Kōkei, Benzaiten is the third daughter of the dragon-king of Munetsuchi (無熱池; literally "lake without heat"), known in Sanskrit as {{ill|Anavatapta|en|Anavatapta|qid=Q4752395|lt=Anavatapta|1=Anavatapta}}, the lake lying at the center of the world according to an ancient Buddhist cosmological view. [[Ryōhō-ji]], also known as the "Moe Temple", enshrines Benzaiten. It is famous for {{ill|Anime|en|Anime|qid=Q1107|lt=Anime|1=Anime}} style depictions of Buddhist deities.{{cite web | url=https://otakuusamagazine.com/otaku-worship-in-session-at-ryohoji-temple/ | title=Otaku Worship in Session at Ryohoji Temple | date=30 January 2013 |qq=}} ===Benzaiten Buddhist temples=== *[[Hogon-ji]] Temple (Nagahama City, Shiga Prefecture, Chikubushima, Japan's Three Major Benzaiten) *[[Daigan-ji]] Temple (Hyokkaichi City, Hiroshima Prefecture, Miyajima, Japan's three major Bensaiten) *[[Yaotomi Shrine]] (Gamagori City, Aichi Prefecture, Takeshima (Aichi Prefecture), Japan Shichibenten) *[[Enkyo-ji Temple]] (Himeji City, Hyōgo Prefecture, on the day of Kishi every 60 days Uga Benzai Tenyu Shuku is practiced) *[[Takian-ji]] Temple (Mino City, Osaka Prefecture) *[[Shinju-ji]] Temple (Nishinomiya City, Hyōgo Prefecture) *[[Shinfuku-ji]] Temple (Tsuyama City, Okayama Prefecture) *{{ill|Tōkai-ji (Chiba)|en|Tōkai-ji (Chiba)|qid=Q1000448|lt=Tokai-ji|1=Tōkai-ji (Chiba)}} Temple Fuse Benten (Kashiwa City, Chiba Prefecture) *[[:en:Senso-ji|Senso-ji]] Temple Bentenzan (Taito Ward, Tokyo) [11] *[[:en:Kanei-ji|Kanei-ji]] Temple Shinobazu Pond Benzaiten (Taito Ward, Tokyo, Edo Shichibenten) *[[Myoko-ji]] Temple Itsukushima Bensaiten (Ueda City, Nagano Prefecture, Kamehime-sama's dedication) *[[Togo-ji]] Temple (Nagoya City, Aichi Prefecture) *[[:en:Kofuku-ji|Kofuku-ji]] Temple Kubo Benzaiten (Nara City, Nara Prefecture, in the three-storied pagoda, hidden Buddha) *[[Ryozen-ji]] Temple Daibensai Tendo (Nara City, Nara Prefecture) *[[Chokenji]] Temple (Fushimi Ward, Kyoto City) *Fukasawa Zenarai Benten (Tonosawa, Hakone-cho, Ashigashimo-gun, Kanagawa Prefecture) *[[Saifuku-ji]] Temple (Kagoshima City, Kagoshima Prefecture, Japan's largest wooden Buddha) *[[Ikko-ji]] Temple Benten Cave (Inagi City, Tokyo) *[[Daisei-ji]] Temple Inokashira Benzaiten (Mitaka City, Tokyo) *[[Honko-ji]] Temple, Suse Benzai Kotoku Tenjo (Ichikawa City, Chiba Prefecture) *Hase-dera Bentendo Benten Cave (Kamakura City, Kanagawa Prefecture) *[[Kaiko-ji]] Temple, Izumiyama Yutsu Benzaiten (Yamauchi of Senwakuji Temple, Higashiyama Ward, Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture, Hachibi statue with Denkyo Daishi Saicho Saku, Hidden Buddha) *[[Ryōhō-ji]] Temple Shingo Benzaiten (Hachioji City, Tokyo, dating from 1489, known as "Moe-ji Temple" for its use of anime style depictions of Buddhist deities) *[[Momo-ji]] Temple Naked Benzaiten (2-16 Yotsuya-dori, Chikusa-ku, Nagoya City) *There is a shrine on a floating island protruding in Tanara-numa, [[Onrin-ji]] Temple (Oura-cho, Gunma Prefecture). *[[Eian-ji]] Temple (Kanazawa City, Ishikawa Prefecture) Enshrined in Bentendo where vermilion Hiten dances with Bishamonten and Daikokuten (opened several times a year) *[[Myoen-ji]] Temple Iwaya Reijo Tsuchiya Zeni Benten (Hiratsuka City, Kanagawa Prefecture) *Jushoin Matsumoto Benten (Edogawa-ku, Tokyo) *[[Hoju-in]] Kaiun Suzusato-saiten (Minato-ku, Tokyo) *[[Meio-ji]] Temple (Ibaraki City, Osaka Prefecture, Benten sect headquarters) *[[Nyoi-ji]] Temple (the head temple of the Benten sect in Gojo City, Nara Prefecture, Gyoki Bodhisattva is said to have been carved in the Nara period) ===Shinto shrines enshrining Benzaiten=== *Enoshima Shrine (Fujisawa City, Kanagawa Prefecture, Enoshima, Japan's three major Benzaiten) *Koami Shrine (Nihonbashi, Tokyo) *Zeniarai Benzaiten Ugafuku Shrine (Kamakura City, Kanagawa Prefecture) *Shimizu Benzaitensha (Saku City, Nagano Prefecture) *Tenkawa Daibenzaitensha (Tenkawa-mura, Yoshino-gun, Nara Prefecture, Japan's three major Benzaiten) [Note 6] *Koganeyama Shrine (Ishinomaki City, Miyagi Prefecture, Mt. Kinka) Benzaiten is also enshrined as Ichikishima Hime-no-Mikoto at the [[Munakata Taisha]] shrine. ==See also== * [[Japanese Buddhist pantheon#Level 4: Heavenly deities (Ten-bu)|Buddhist Tenbu (天部) deities]] * [[Daikokuten]] * [[Dakini]] * {{ill|Hinduism in Japan|en|Hinduism in Japan|qid=Q17049486|lt=Hinduism in Japan|1=Hinduism in Japan}} * [[Seven Lucky Gods]] * [[Shinbutsu-shūgō]] * {{ill|Three Great Shrines of Benzaiten|en|Three Great Shrines of Benzaiten|qid=Q17214976|lt=Three Great Shrines of Benzaiten|1=Three Great Shrines of Benzaiten}} * {{ill|Tenjin (kami)|en|Tenjin (kami)|qid=Q1753428|lt=Tenjin (kami)|1=Tenjin (kami)}} * {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|qid=Q866315|lt=Vaiśravaṇa|1=Vaiśravaṇa}} * {{ill|Anahita|en|Anahita|qid=Q484278|lt=Anahita|1=Anahita}} ==References== {{Reflist|2|qq=}} ==Bibliography== *{{cite book |last1=Faure |first1=Bernard |title=Protectors and Predators: Gods of Medieval Japan, Volume 2 |date=2015 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0824857721|qq=}} *{{cite book |last1=Kinsley |first1=David |title=Hindu Goddesses: Visions of the Divine Feminine in the Hindu Religious Tradition |date=1998 |publisher=Motilal Banarsidass Publishers |isbn=978-8-1208-0394-7|qq=}} *{{cite journal |last1=Ludvik |first1=Catherine |title=A Harivaṃśa Hymn in Yijing's Chinese Translation of the Sutra of Golden Light |journal=Journal of the American Oriental Society |date=2004 |volume=124 |issue=4 |pages=707–734 |doi=10.2307/4132114 |jstor=4132114 |url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/4132114|qq=}} *{{cite book |last1=Ludvik |first1=Catherine |title=Sarasvatī: Riverine Goddess of Knowledge. From the Manuscript-carrying Vīṇā-player to the Weapon-wielding Defender of the Dharma |date=2007 |publisher=Brill |isbn=978-9-0474-2036-1|qq=}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Benzaiten|qq=}} *[https://sites.google.com/site/bemsha10/intro A Study of the Enoshima Engi] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161219073950/https://sites.google.com/site/bemsha10/intro |date=2016-12-19 |qq=}} *[http://www.sacred-texts.com/journals/oc/ts-sgb.htm Sacred Texts account of Benzaiten] {{jmyth navbox long|qq=}} {{Buddhist Pantheon|qq=}} {{Asia in topic|Hinduism in|qq=}} {{Authority control|qq=}} [[wiktionary:弁才天|弁才天]] [[wiktionary:弁財天|弁財天]] [[en:Benzaiten]] [[ar:بينزايتين]] [[bcl:Benzaiten]] [[bn:বেন্‌যাইতেন]] {{Commons category|Benzaiten}} [[de:Benzaiten]] [[el:Μπενζάιτεν]] [[es:Benzaiten]] [[eu:Benzaiten]] [[fr:Benzaiten]] [[he:בנזייטן]] [[hi:बेंज़ाइतेन]] [[hr:Benten]] [[id:Benzaiten]] [[it:Benzaiten]] [[ja:弁才天]] [[ko:변재천]] [[mni:ꯕꯦꯟꯓꯥꯢꯇꯦꯟ]] [[ms:Benzaiten]] [[pl:Benzaiten]] [[ru:Бэндзайтэн]] [[sh:Benzaiten]] [[simple:Benzaiten]] [[sv:Benten]] [[ta:பென்சைட்டென்]] [[th:เบ็นไซเต็ง]] [[tr:Benzaiten]] [[uk:Бендзайтен]] [[zh:辯才天]] {{wikidata link|Q818468}} [[Category:Seven Lucky Gods]] [[Category:Toilet deities]] [[Category:Commerce deities]] [[Category:天部]] [[Category:Goddesses]] [[Category:Vajrayana]] [[Category:Japanese Vajrayana Buddhism]] [[Category:Music history of Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Meiji era]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Arts deities]] [[Category:Dharmapalas]] [[Category:Music history]]
